title: Canadians’ knowledge and attitudes around drug decriminalization: Results from a 2025 public opinion research survey - Canada.ca
content:
## Canadians’ knowledge and attitudes around drug decriminalization: Results from a 2025 public opinion research survey
Date published: April 24, 2026
- Background on the exemption in British Columbia related to personal possession of certain illegal drugs
- Approach to monitoring attitudes around drug decriminalization
- About the 2025 Public Opinion Research survey
- Key highlights from the 2025 POR survey results

## Background on the exemption in British Columbia related to personal possession of certain illegal drugs
At the request of the Government of British Columbia, Health Canada issued an exemption related to personal possession of certain illegal drugs for adults 18 years of age and older in the province. The exemption came into effect on January 31, 2023. The exemption expired on January 31, 2026. British Columbia (BC) has indicated that they are not seeking a renewal. BC refers to this exemption as a form of decriminalization.
Under the original exemption, adults 18 years of age and older could not be subject to criminal charges or drug seizure if they were found in personal possession of up to 2.5 grams total of any combination of:
Instead, they were offered information about health and social supports. This includes support with referrals to local treatment and recovery services, if requested.
As of May 7, 2024, the details of the exemption related to personal possession in BC were amended. Possession was prohibited in public spaces, including transit, while exemptions remained in place for private residences, designated healthcare clinics, places where people were lawfully sheltering, and overdose prevention and drug checking sites. Since this public opinion research (POR) was conducted in July 2025, the results relate to the scope of the exemption after this amendment. Previous years of POR research (2023 and 2024) relate to the original scope of the amendment.

## Approach to monitoring attitudes around drug decriminalization
Health Canada collected data at a national level on Canadians' attitudes towards, and knowledge of, drug decriminalization. To do this, Health Canada identified a set of indicators to monitor changes related to:
- attitudes towards drug use
- perceptions of public safety
A key source of data for this work was information gathered from POR. POR surveys were conducted annually by Health Canada throughout the exemption period.

## About the 2025 Public Opinion Research survey
The POR survey was conducted by the Privy Council Office (PCO). It used a random sample of 2,000 Canadian adults aged 18 and older. The survey was conducted between July 14 and July 27, 2025. Respondents were randomly sampled with proportional representation from across Canada. Unless otherwise noted, results shown in this report are expressed as percentages. They may not add up to 100% due to rounding or a “not sure” response to a given question.
Questions focused on attitudes and knowledge about:
- preferences for approaches to addressing substance use, including a focus on health and social services versus police enforcement
- general attitudes around empathy for people struggling with substance use
- general attitudes around level of comfort in talking to friends and family members about their substance use
- perceived benefits and disadvantages of decriminalization This included a focus on improved access to relevant health and social services, reduced stigma, increased harms, and reduced community safety
- This included a focus on improved access to relevant health and social services, reduced stigma, increased harms, and reduced community safety
The POR survey also included 4 true-or-false questions to test respondents' knowledge of the details of BC's exemption for personal possession.
The 2025 POR survey was the third POR survey conducted on BC's exemption. The first POR survey was conducted in 2023 and the second POR survey in 2024. In addition to sharing findings from the 2025 POR survey, this report will highlight how responses have changed since 2023 and 2024.
In 2025, the 4 knowledge questions were replaced with updated questions to reflect the changes made to the exemption in 2024. A new question was also added, asking whether respondents were aware of BC's decriminalization policy. Therefore, the knowledge questions in 2025 are not comparable to questions from previous POR surveys.

## Key highlights from the 2025 POR survey results
To address substance use, Canadians preferred a focus on access to health and social services (39%) rather than police enforcement (13%). 40% felt that both approaches were equally appropriate, and 6% felt that neither approach was appropriate ( Figure 1 ).
A preference for focusing on health and social services dropped by 5 percent compared to the 2024 POR and by 10 percent compared to the 2023 POR ( Figure 1 ). Support for police enforcement increased by 7 percent compared to 2024 and by 6 percent compared to 2023 ( Figure 1 ).
Those more likely to prefer a focus on access to health and social services ( Figure 2a ) were:
- residents of BC (43%) and the Atlantic provinces (44%)
- people with university education (44%)
- non-immigrant respondents (42%)
- people with a trade or college education (39%)
- people with no kids under the age of 18 (40%)
- residents of urban areas (40%)
Those more likely to prefer a focus on police enforcement ( Figure 2b ) were:
- people with a high school education or less (17%)
- people from ages 18 to 34 (16%)
Those with kids under the age of 18 (45%) and residents of rural areas (48%) were more likely to think that both approaches (access to health and social services and police enforcement) were equally appropriate ( Figure 2c ).
Most Canadians would like their friends or family members who use drugs to feel comfortable talking to them about it (84%) and have empathy for people struggling with drug use (78%; Figure 3 ). However, agreement with both of these statements has decreased slightly compared to 2023, by 4 and 3 percent respectively ( Figure 4 ).
Half of Canadians believed that decriminalization would make it easier to access health and social services. However, agreement with this statement has dropped by 7 percent compared to 2024 and by 12 percent compared to 2023 ( Figure 4 ). In 2025, 43% believed decriminalization would reduce stigma towards people who use drugs. Agreement with this statement decreased by 7 percent compared to 2024 and by 13 percent compared to 2023 ( Figure 4 ). The belief that drug decriminalization would make communities less safe has not changed since 2024 (48%), but has increased by 5 percent since 2023 ( Figure 4 ).
Focus on access to health and social services such as drug treatment centres and recovery programs
Both approaches are equally appropriate
Focus on police enforcement such as criminal charges and jail time
Neither approach is appropriate

Source: PCO Survey on Current Issues, February 27 to March 12, 2023; February 12 to 25, 2024; July 14 to 27, 2025 Base: All respondents (Canada: n = 2,000 in 2023; n = 2,000 in 2024; n = 2,000 in 2025) Note: Responses for 2023 and 2024 reflect participants' attitudes prior to the amendment of the exemption in May 2024
Half of Canadians were aware of BC's decriminalization policy.
Residents of BC (74%), Alberta (57%), and Saskatchewan (60%) had the highest awareness of BC's policy relative to other provinces. Those more likely to be aware of BC's decriminalization policy ( Figure 5 ) were:
- people with household income over $150,000 (60%)
- people with household income between $60,000 and $100,000 (56%)
- non-immigrants (54%)
- people with university education (52%)
- people with college education (51%)

Question: Since January 31, 2023, British Columbia has had a decriminalization policy. Were you aware that British Columbia had adopted this policy? Source: PCO Survey on Current Issues, July 14 to 27, 2025 Base: All respondents (Canada: n = 2,000) Note: Responses reflect participants' attitudes after the amendment of the exemption in May 2024
Questions testing Canadians' knowledge of the exemption in BC focused on:
- details about which activities involving drugs are covered by the exemption
- spaces where possession is permitted or prohibited under the amended exemption
Between 43% to 66% of Canadians, depending on the question, were able to correctly identify details of BC's exemption ( Table 1 ). However, 23% to 40% of respondents answered incorrectly and 12% to 18% were unsure of the answer. Many Canadians incorrectly believed people cannot be charged with possession if they are holding under 2.5 grams of drugs on a sidewalk or at a park (40%).
Percent who answered “True”
Percent who answered “False”
Percent who answered “Not sure”
People can be charged with possession if they are holding under 2.5 grams of drugs at a supervised consumption site or a drug checking site
People can be charged with possession if they are holding under 2.5 grams of drugs on a sidewalk or at a park
People can be arrested for drug trafficking/dealing, no matter how much drug they are selling or trading
People can be charged with breach of substance-related release conditions no matter how much they are holding
Question: Based on your understanding of the decriminalization of personal possession of drugs being implemented in British Columbia, are the following statements true or false? Source: PCO Survey on Current Issues, July 14 to 27, 2025 Base: All respondents (Canada: n = 2,000) Note: Questions reflect participants' knowledge after the amendment of the exemption in May 2024
Question: Based on your understanding of the decriminalization of personal possession of drugs being implemented in British Columbia, are the following statements true or false? Source: PCO Survey on Current Issues, July 14 to 27, 2025 Base: All respondents (Canada: n = 2,000) Note: Questions reflect participants' knowledge after the amendment of the exemption in May 2024
The results from this survey reflect the knowledge and attitudes of Canadian adults around drug decriminalization in BC in 2025 and serve as an update to the 2023 and 2024 results. They are important for monitoring key outcomes of the exemption, particularly public awareness and understanding of the details of the exemption and substance use. Health Canada's work compliments monitoring and evaluation work being led by the BC Ministry of Health and the Canadian Institutes of Health Research.
